Price Relativity, the Halbert 15 step marketing template and on being a how-artologist. Hi, it's Kevin Halbert. In our last video we talked about how to start writing your copy and you probably realize there's a lot more to telling a persuasive story than you thought. Today, we're going to discuss the mechanics of how it actually creates more business for you. We're also going to talk about price and how it is relative and how to exploit perceived value to increase your bottom line. Let's start with pricing. The most important thing I can ever tell you about pricing is the same thing my dad, Gary Halbert, yelled about in his newsletters. Price is relative. But what does that mean? Does it mean you can price your product at double or even triple the market and expect to make sales? It can if you have built a brand that your customers equate with perceived value. What is perceived value? Let's go back to our very first video when we talked about creating your story. That the words you use matter because they create meaning for your customer. That's what perceived value is, the meaning beyond the specific product or service that your customer takes from using your product. Remember the Volvo versus Ferrari example? That's perceived value. For Volvo buyers the value lies in safety but a Ferrari sells for a lot more money because its perceived value gives the buyer status. Once again we find ourselves back at Maslow's pyramid finding the need we're meeting that is beyond the basic. Let's go back to our water example. That water, the same water you could get from a tap, a river, an iceberg fed mountain stream or a rain barrel sells for $219 a bottle. Granted that bottle has a real gold plated cap but it's not claiming to cure cancer, first aging or anything else. The water has nothing to do with why a customer pays 129 times the value of a regular bottle of water. It's the perceived value. A customer who would buy the $219 bottle wants to prove that he could throw money away on anything he wants. The demographic targets for this water are the same ones who spent $5,000 on a shower curtain and $500,000 on a car. And again the top of the pyramid value is what commands top dollar, status. When you're selling status the sky could be the limit. You just have to break through the customer's level of price resistance, what your customer thinks something should cost. Here's what dad had to say about it. And so it is with points of price resistance. What I'm trying to illuminate here is that once you break through a certain level of price resistance you may as well go all the way to the next resistance point. For example, a common price point in mail order ads is $19.95. Now most of my experience tells me that if you're going above $19.95 you may as well go all the way to $39.95 and after $39.95 you may as well go all the way to $69.95. I'm not going to try in such limited time to cover the specifics of price points. But know that there is a difference of opinion about pricing, especially when creating what are sometimes called tripwires, which we'll cover in the main course. The debate centers around whether it's better to get your customers to say yes to a smaller price point item first to pave the way for the yes on the big ticket item later. There are some marketers who swear by low priced early offers and some who would never use them. Which leads me to dad's next thought on price relativity. The most profitable price you can charge varies dramatically depending on how sold your prospects are. Do you need to pre-sell them with a tripwire offer or is a simple opt in enough? At what price point is a tripwire unnecessary if it's ever needed at all? And the kicker, the colonel of howbert truth on the matter, a lukewarm sales job forces you to charge lukewarm prices. So if you do a better job of selling, of tapping perceived value, storytelling and making your offer you could charge more money. Yes. That's the reason we're here, right? To figure out how to make more money. So let's do it right now. While there may be more to writing great copy than you realized, I want to make something perfectly clear. I believe that nearly anyone could write copy that motivates customers to take action. Sure, a dash of talent and charisma doesn't hurt, but marketing is just like anything in life that's worth doing. It takes some effort. It's a craft. You wouldn't expect to perform open heart surgery after an hour's worth of instruction, would you? Then why would you think you could write killer copy, drive the world to your door and exponentially grow your business and your bottom line after a couple of days? What we're doing with howbertology is giving you the tools you need to succeed. Those tools will allow you to be light years ahead of your competition when it comes to creating a successful marketing strategy, growing your business and blowing the roof off of your sales. It is possible to learn to do nearly anything in life if you have four things. A clearly defined goal, a roadmap with step by step instructions for how to get there, a mentor and the will to succeed. You have that will or you wouldn't be here with me right now. That thirst for knowledge, the quest to start or improve your business is in your blood. Howbertologists do it different. Mark often just sent me that and I swear I'm going to put it on a t-shirt. Entrepreneurs know we're not like other people. Legendary entrepreneurs like my dad take that knowledge of being different and use it to create wildly successful businesses. That same blood runs through your veins too because you're here, chomping at the bit to learn everything you need to take your business to the next level. It's like Mark often says, people either have the life force or they don't. I believe that you do and that's why you're still watching. The roadmap or at least the beginning of it is what I've been sharing with you over these short videos. My goal when we're done is to know that you will have more marketing tools in your toolbox than 90% of all the business owners in the world. One of the things that makes howbertology different is that I'm not the only person who will be sharing proven market tested strategies with you. You will be on the inside getting to pick the brains of many of the best in our business. For me, there's nothing better than that. Finding someone who has succeeded at what you want to do and learning from them how they do it is one of the best paths to success. Role models are everywhere and in howbertology you'll find a full boat of marketing and copy giants who are willing to share all of their secrets with you. Sorry, I didn't mean to stray too far into the future. Mark and I are just so pumped about this course. Anyway, I brought up role models because they can become mentors. You might think that a mentor isn't essential to learning anything, but let me ask you this. When you went to school and learned reading, writing, arithmetic, and all of that, how did you do it? Someone taught you how. Teachers are amazing people, aren't they? The best ones are patient and kind, but also disciplined. If you call Tiger Woods' dad his coach, his teacher, or his mentor, he taught him to be an amazing golfer. The same goes for the coaches of tennis greats venus and serena williams, olympian michael felps, and superstar footballer david beckham. Each athlete has reached the very top of their respective sports and none of them could have done it alone. I never aspired to teaching as a profession, but I am so stoked about offering this course that I decided I had to do it. The reason you are here today is because Gary Halbert did teach people and his work is still teaching us today. He created his Gary Halbert letters to not only sell products, but to teach his fans how to do it. So let's get down to it. Copy is not just sales letters and pages. Copy is the script of your favorite ad, YouTube video, radio commercial, and so much more. Store displays, newspaper circulars, product packaging, even the one ads. All of it is copy. Even if you came into this thinking you couldn't write a successful sales letter, I assure you you can. Maybe you won't use the traditional format. Maybe your specialty is in video scripts or packaging or product descriptions. The potential is as endless as your imagination, but the process of making the sale is the same no matter what the medium. Like david franklin said, marketing is marketing. The only thing that changes is the medium. When considering copy in your sales process, it is imperative to remember that it's everywhere and that to create a compelling message, all of your copy for any offer needs to be consistent. I've taught you a little bit of what I have time for about market research, crafting a story that resonates with your customer, and how your product benefits that customer by fulfilling a desire or solving a problem. Now we're going to get into the heart of the beast, why copy works. One of the greatest copywriters of all time was David Ogilvy. His agency Ogilvy and Mather is still making millions long after his death on the strength of his amazing marketing and copy craft. How much are they making? How does 1.4 billion dollars sound? That's a lot of successful copy, but here's the good news. No matter if you're David Ogilvy, Gary Halbert, or you in front of your computer, the nuts and bolts of great copy do not change. Oh sure, there are innovators, but even Ogilvy didn't invent his sales letter offer, he swiped it from Lewis Engle. Engle did it in 1948 with one of the swipes we discussed in the last video, what everybody should know about this stock and bond business. What Ogilvy did was to perfect the offer, and most great copywriters and marketers still use something like it to this day. Isn't it great to know that giants like Ogilvy and my dad just used the tools they had available, learned to perfect their craft, and made millions. They also put their pants on one leg at a time too, just like you. Whether you consider great copy and art, a science, or a craft, most of it has a basic structure and I'm going to uncover the bare bones on which all great copy is made today. Okay, so you've done your market research, your fact and benefit sheets, and come up with a pretty good offer. How do you translate that into copy that sells? Copy that has your customer screaming to get to the buy now button before it's too late. There are lots of ways to break it down, but I'm going to give you the 15 secrets to great copy structure. Dad liked to use a car as an example. One, you create a fact sheet. Two, you create a benefit list. Three, reduce your offer to writing on a deal sheet. Four, you write a headline that contains news and the promise of one or more benefits. Five, if you have more than two hot benefits, you can put another in the lead in sub headline. Six, you reveal exactly who the letter is from. Seven, you begin the salutation. Dear friend. Eight, you write a first sentence that tells what's in it for your prospect if you read this letter. Nine, use the next few paragraphs to elaborate specifically on your promises. Ten, you next tell them why you are offering such a good deal. Eleven, you enumerate facts that translate to benefits and you simply list one, two, three, four, etc., etc., as many as you've got. Twelve, you next write a true and believable reason why explanation of why you are willing to part with this wonderful car. Thirteen, you ask him to respond and urge him to hurry. Fourteen, you sign off with sincerely. Fifteen, you write a PS that gives him a little new wrinkle on one of the hot reasons you've already given him as to why he should buy this car now. If you are able to write convincingly and structure your online or print copy with these items, you could increase sales very quickly. We have already covered the fact and benefits sheets in the last video, but let's recap. One, the fact sheet is every attribute great and small of your product or service. Two, the benefit list is the way each attribute can become a benefit for your customer. I'll discuss a little on each of the others and a whole lot on one that I believe to be the most important. Going down the list, we next have three, the deal sheet. This is where you craft your offer, make it straightforward and then ask yourself, how could you sweeten the deal? Could you offer more? Add value, bonuses, perks, write it all out and be sure you know what you're going to give for what you get. Four, the headline. This is the biggie. One of the most surefire ways to increase sales is to write an excellent headline. Nearly all marketing experts agree that the headline determines 80% of your sales. This is absolutely true. Do you know the Pareto principle? It says that 20% of your action is responsible for 80% of your results. Just like Maslow's pyramid, we find the Pareto principle to be right on target. Your headline might make up only 20% or even less of your copy, but it's the first moment your market will encounter your message, your voice, and it does 80% of the heavy lifting for you. If the headline is that important, you'd better learn how to craft a fantastic one, right? Right. A few caveats to great headline writing. It's not like writing a newspaper headline, leaving out articles, prepositions, or writing only a couple of words will not fly for marketing. In fact, some very long headlines have made a lot of sales. It is true that the prevailing wisdom of eight words or less can be effective, but only if it conveys everything your headline needs to tell your customer. If you need more words to get your point across, then use them, but use them wisely. It should make a big promise with a huge benefit, possibly the biggest benefit you can think of. Inviting the reader to learn how to do something is one of the most effective headline openings you can write. People are curious beasts and want to learn stuff. Remember to keep the language simple. We are writing to that fifth grade level. It should never have an end stop, full stop. You might be tempted when making a big benefit claim to throw an exclamation point at the end or to phrase it like a question. Don't. It can be long, include commas, dashes, hyphens, ellipses, but no end stop. Putting it in quotes increases viewership. Yes, I know that you aren't quoting anyone in your headline, but somehow people will respond more to something in quotes. A larger point size is fine and bolding works too, but if you're going to add color, be sure to decide what color works best. Some marketers like red in headlines, while others feel blue pulls better. That's just seven points on writing a headline, and I've barely scratched the surface of what it can do. But, if you only remember those seven things, your headlines will improve a lot. And improving the part of your marketing responsible for 80% of your sales could definitely make you more money. On to the other basic parts of copy. 5. Hot Benefits Slash Subhead. You can add an additional benefit that could be headline worthy on its own as the start of the copy, included in the first subhead. We'll discuss those in just a minute. 6-12. The Deck. This is the meat of your copy, where you identify the problem or your customers need, tell your story, lay out your product's benefits, provide proof, and make your offer. In the Deck is where you will place items 6-12 on Dad's list. Everything from the opening statement to the offer fall into the Deck. 6. Let them know who is writing. It might not be you, but could be your product's creator, a character, even your pet, if it hooks the reader. Voice matters here, a lot. 7. Salutation. If you're doing a DR mail piece, you could have a traditional salutation, but in I am, it's more than likely a shout out to your intended audience. Attention all weather balloon enthusiasts, or calling all bike messengers. You get the idea. Your first sentence, like your headline, has to knock it out of the park. You have to identify the big problem and give them some idea that you have exactly the answer to solve it. 9. Your first few paragraphs must expand on the idea of the problem and why your product is the solution. 10. When you go on to explain how you are able to make the offer you are about to make or have just made. Usually, this is where you can build value as well. 11. Features and benefits come next. Be sure you understand the difference. A feature is what something is. A benefit is what it can do for your customer. 12. Why are you making your offer? It must be sincere and believable. Are you trying to change the world? Enjoy helping others to be successful? Or a favorite, sticking it to an authority by doing something that upsets the status quo? Whatever your reason, remember that proof is the thing your customers want to see most. Proof that it will do what you promised it would in your headline. The purpose of your deck is to keep the reader reading until he makes up his mind to buy. Or to make him leave your page as fast as you can. A truism of marketing is that if you aren't offending someone, you're not doing it right. We don't like turning potential customers off. But like I've said before, getting the look you lose to take a hike and fast can be an effective way to secure that you are selling to only the customers most likely to purchase your product. This is an advanced technique though, and I don't recommend it to someone just starting in marketing. The deck has a lot to do, but remember, 80% of your customers make their minds up before even glancing at your deck. However, and this is the most important thing your deck does, it must deliver on the promise you made in your headline. 13. Call to action. This is where you invite your customer to buy. It should be strongly worded, but does not need to scream at them. Everywhere your prospects turn, they are shouted at with new, improved, act now, limited time, operators are standing by. Just tell them what you want them to do and how to do it. 14 and 15. The closing and PS. In a letter, the closing and PS matter a lot. If you are crafting an online sales page, you can format it just like a traditional sales letter you would get in the mail. Many good marketers do just that and close with a traditional regards. What they sometimes miss though, is the importance of that PS. Here's what dad said about it. PS, don't forget to always add a PS, because some people read that part of the letter first. And what the PS should say is some little thing additional about one of the items of hot and ticing info you've already given him. Some marketers use the PS to sum up the features and benefits one last time. And you can craft it however it works best for you, but you need a PS. Okay, so here are a couple of additional bits of information. Bonuses, if you will, to help you on your way to marketing greatness. Subheads. Your subheads are the short, sometimes pithy statements and questions that can act as the opening of each new section of your page. Making these bold is important, because we know from heat mapping studies that the average customer's eyes scan the page and pick out the subheads first. Art and captions. You don't have to include art or photos in your copy, but it can help, especially if you include a photo caption. Captions are another item that customers scan too. Here's an interesting tidbit. Eye gravity, how the eye lands on a page, shows us that captions placed below photos track better than those beside them. A note about photos. If you're going to use photos of things other than your product, spokesperson, or employees, invest in a stock photo service. Otherwise, you will find yourself unintentionally breaking copyright laws by using content without paying for it or crediting the source. You don't want to ruin a fantastic pitch by using a photo you have no right to use. Ken Foodie once said that in a sales offer, you can get 90% of it wrong and only 10% of it right and still make more money than your competition. And anyone can get 10% of it right, yes? So you have nothing to worry about. But if I'm doing my job, you'll get a lot more than 10% right. I have covered a ton with you today, so I think we'll put it all together and wrap it up next time. See you soon.
